title: PostgreSQL
objectives: Understand PostgreSQL via a real-life analogy
Master the features that make it stronger than MySQL — JSON, arrays, GIN/GiST indexes
Use MVCC transactions correctly (isolation, VACUUM, bloat)
Handle full-text search, extensions, and JSON queries

sections.markdown:
**Think of PostgreSQL as a Swiss Army knife database — one tool that does 80% of everything specialized databases do.**

- Need a **relational DB**? Postgres. Rock-solid SQL, ACID, correct semantics.
- Need to store and query **JSON documents** like MongoDB? Postgres has `jsonb` with indexed queries.
- Need **arrays**? Native array types with operators.
- Need **full-text search**? Built in (`tsvector` + `tsquery`).
- Need **geospatial**? PostGIS extension — one of the world's best GIS databases.
- Need **time-series**? TimescaleDB extension.
- Need a **key-value store**? `hstore`.
- Need **UUIDs, ranges, IP addresses, geometric types**? Native types.
- Need to **extend the query language with your own function types**? User-defined functions in PL/pgSQL, PL/Python, PL/Rust.

**One database can replace three or four specialized ones.** That's why Postgres is the default for modern greenfield projects — you can start with 'just Postgres' and only add specialized DBs when you outgrow what Postgres does. Netflix, Instagram, Reddit, Apple, Discord, Twitter (portions), Robinhood — all serious Postgres shops.

**Trade-off vs MySQL:** Postgres is more feature-rich and follows SQL standards more strictly; MySQL is simpler to operate at massive scale and has broader shared-hosting support. For a new backend in 2026, **default to Postgres unless you have a specific MySQL reason.**

**The one Postgres quirk to know:** **MVCC + VACUUM**. Postgres keeps OLD row versions on disk until VACUUM cleans them up (usually via autovacuum). Poorly tuned autovacuum on a write-heavy workload → tables bloat, queries slow. Every production Postgres DBA has spent time tuning it.

**PostgreSQL** (aka 'Postgres') is a free, open-source, object-relational database (1986 at UC Berkeley; open-sourced 1996). Known for correctness, extensibility, and rich type system. Powers Instagram, Reddit, Discord, Apple's iCloud, Netflix's core services. **Storage:** row-based with MVCC (multi-version concurrency control). **Transactions:** full ACID; default isolation is READ COMMITTED. **Rich types:** JSON/JSONB, arrays, ranges, UUID, geometric, network addresses. **Extensions:** PostGIS (GIS), TimescaleDB (time-series), pg_vector (embeddings), pg_partman (partitioning). **Client:** SQL over port 5432. Managed variants: AWS RDS/Aurora, GCP Cloud SQL, Azure Database, Supabase, Neon, Crunchy Data.
## The features that separate Postgres from MySQL

rows: `jsonb` + GIN index — full JSON path queries at scale
B-tree, Hash, GIN, GiST, SP-GiST, BRIN, Bloom
PL/pgSQL, PL/Python, PL/Rust, custom types, extensions

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — a modern Postgres schema

sections.code:
-- ============ Schema with proper types ============
CREATE TABLE users (
  id           BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  email        TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  name         TEXT NOT NULL,
  metadata     JSONB DEFAULT '{}'::jsonb,           -- flexible structured data
  tags         TEXT[] DEFAULT '{}',                  -- array
  created_at   TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
  is_active    BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE
);

-- Multiple index types on the same table for different use cases:
CREATE INDEX idx_email ON users (email);              -- B-tree — fast equality/range
CREATE INDEX idx_created ON users (created_at DESC);  -- B-tree with sort direction
CREATE INDEX idx_metadata_gin ON users USING GIN (metadata);  -- JSON queries
CREATE INDEX idx_tags_gin ON users USING GIN (tags);            -- array containment

CREATE TABLE orders (
  id           BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id      BIGINT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
  items        JSONB NOT NULL,                        -- store line items as JSON
  total_cents  BIGINT NOT NULL CHECK (total_cents > 0),
  status       TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (status IN ('pending', 'paid', 'shipped', 'cancelled')),
  created_at   TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_created ON orders (user_id, created_at DESC);

-- ============ SQL features you'll actually use ============

-- Query JSON fields — direct SQL on JSONB
SELECT id, name, metadata->>'city' AS city, metadata->'preferences'->>'theme' AS theme
FROM   users
WHERE  metadata @> '{"role": "admin"}';               -- JSONB containment

-- Query arrays
SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE 'premium' = ANY(tags);
SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE tags @> ARRAY['premium', 'verified'];  -- both required

-- CTE (WITH clause) + window function
WITH order_ranks AS (
  SELECT o.*,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY total_cents DESC) AS rank_in_user
  FROM   orders o
)
SELECT u.id, u.name, o.total_cents
FROM   users u
JOIN   order_ranks o ON o.user_id = u.id
WHERE  o.rank_in_user <= 3;                            -- top 3 orders per user

-- Full-text search
CREATE INDEX idx_users_search ON users USING GIN (to_tsvector('english', name));
SELECT id, name FROM users
WHERE  to_tsvector('english', name) @@ plainto_tsquery('english', 'ada lovelace');

-- Upsert with RETURNING (much cleaner than MySQL)
INSERT INTO users (email, name) VALUES ('ada@x.com', 'Ada')
ON CONFLICT (email) DO UPDATE SET name = EXCLUDED.name
RETURNING id, created_at;

-- Modern date/time
SELECT id, created_at, created_at AT TIME ZONE 'UTC' AS utc_time,
       AGE(NOW(), created_at) AS age
FROM orders WHERE created_at >= NOW() - INTERVAL '7 days';

sections.markdown: ## MVCC + VACUUM — the one thing to understand

sections.code:
Postgres uses MULTI-VERSION CONCURRENCY CONTROL (MVCC):

  Every UPDATE creates a NEW ROW VERSION; the old version stays for readers.
  Every DELETE marks a row as deleted; the old row stays for readers.

Why: enables concurrent reads and writes without blocking readers.
  Readers see a consistent snapshot from their transaction's start.
  Writers create new versions; readers never block writers, writers never
  block readers (for reads). Beautiful concurrency model.

CONSEQUENCE: dead rows accumulate. Table size grows even as data churns.

VACUUM reclaims space from dead rows:
  - AUTOVACUUM runs continuously in the background. Usually fine at default.
  - Under HIGH write volume, autovacuum falls behind → table bloat.
  - Bloat = table on disk is 2-10x the size of live rows. Queries scan bloat.

Monitor bloat:
  SELECT relname, pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size(oid)) AS size
  FROM pg_class WHERE relkind = 'r' ORDER BY pg_total_relation_size(oid) DESC LIMIT 10;

Tune autovacuum for hot tables (in postgresql.conf or per-table):
  ALTER TABLE orders SET (autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.05);   -- more aggressive

Emergency: VACUUM FULL rewrites the table (locks it). Or pg_repack for online rebuild.

This is the ONE Postgres operational quirk you must understand.

sections.markdown: ## Common mistakes
**Mistake 1: Using `JSON` instead of `JSONB`.** `JSON` stores text as-is (slow to query). `JSONB` is binary + indexable. Almost always `JSONB`.
**Mistake 2: `TIMESTAMP` without `TIMESTAMPTZ`.** `TIMESTAMP` has no timezone → ambiguous. `TIMESTAMPTZ` stores as UTC internally, converts on read. Always `TIMESTAMPTZ`.
**Mistake 3: `INTEGER` for IDs on high-volume tables.** 2.1 billion cap → runs out. Use `BIGINT` / `BIGSERIAL` / `IDENTITY BIGINT`.
**Mistake 4: Ignoring autovacuum tuning.** Write-heavy table + default autovacuum settings = bloat + slow queries. Monitor + tune per-table.
**Mistake 5: `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM huge_table`.** Full table scan — slow on billions of rows. Use approximate counts (`pg_class.reltuples`) or maintain a counter.
**Mistake 6: `CREATE INDEX` on a huge live table.** Locks the table for writes during creation. Use `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` for zero-downtime.

**Mistake 7: Connection pool overhead.** Postgres connections are HEAVY (one process each; ~10MB RAM). Use PgBouncer / built-in connection pooler / your ORM's pool. Direct-app-to-DB with 1000 connections = OOM.
## Best practices
**1. `JSONB` not `JSON`, `TIMESTAMPTZ` not `TIMESTAMP`, `BIGINT` not `INT` for IDs.**
**2. GIN indexes for `JSONB` + arrays + full-text.** The right index for each type.
**3. Connection pooling (PgBouncer) in front of Postgres.** Handle 10,000 client connections with 100 backend connections.
**4. Monitor bloat + autovacuum.** Alert on tables >2x expected size.
**5. `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` — measure real query cost.** Add indexes based on evidence, not guesses.
**6. Migrations via Flyway / Liquibase / Prisma / Alembic.** Version-controlled schema.
**7. Point-in-time recovery via WAL archiving.** Combined with `pg_basebackup` for full backup.
**8. Read replicas with streaming replication.** Read scaling + DR.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Modern schema.** Build the sample; verify JSONB + array queries work.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — JSONB query + index.** `metadata @> '{...}'` with a GIN index; measure with/without index.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Full-text search.** `tsvector` column, GIN index, search with `plainto_tsquery`.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Bloat investigation.** Load 1M rows, update 100k, check for bloat, tune autovacuum, verify.
**Practice 5 (Hard) — Zero-downtime schema change.** `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY`; `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN` with default (Postgres 11+ instant); no lock.
**Practice 6 (Expert) — Streaming replication.** Primary + replica via docker-compose; measure lag under load; test failover.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: Why choose Postgres over MySQL?**

**A:** Both are excellent; the choice depends on team + ecosystem. **Postgres advantages:** (1) **Richer feature set** — advanced index types (GIN, GiST, BRIN, Bloom), correct SQL semantics, extensibility. (2) **Native JSON with indexed queries** (JSONB + GIN). (3) **Arrays, ranges, UUID, geospatial (PostGIS)** as first-class citizens. (4) **CTEs + window functions** since 2009 (MySQL only 2018). (5) **`CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY`** — zero-downtime schema changes. **MySQL advantages:** simpler at massive scale (WhatsApp, YouTube), wider hosting availability, slightly faster for simple queries at high write throughput. **My rule for 2026:** default to Postgres for new projects unless there's a specific MySQL reason. Existing MySQL codebases — keep them; migration is expensive. Both scale to billions of rows in production.

**Q2: What is MVCC and how does Postgres implement it?**

**A:** **MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control)** — instead of locking rows during reads, the database keeps multiple VERSIONS of each row. Every transaction sees a consistent snapshot from its start (or first statement, depending on isolation). **Postgres implementation:** every UPDATE creates a NEW row version; old version stays for concurrent readers. DELETE marks the row obsolete; row stays until VACUUM cleans it. **Benefits:** readers never block writers; writers never block readers (for reads). Excellent concurrency. **Trade-off:** dead row versions accumulate — the **VACUUM** process reclaims them. On write-heavy workloads, autovacuum must be tuned or tables bloat. **MySQL InnoDB also uses MVCC**, but stores old versions in a rollback segment (undo log) instead of in-place. Postgres's approach has different perf characteristics — mostly better but requires VACUUM.

**Q3: What's the difference between JSON and JSONB?**

**A:** **`JSON`** — stores JSON as TEXT verbatim. Preserves whitespace, key order, duplicate keys. Every query re-parses the text. Slower. **`JSONB`** — stores in a decomposed BINARY format. Faster query access. Supports **rich operators**: `@>` (contains), `?` (key exists), `->` (get field), `->>` (get as text). **Indexable with GIN indexes** — `WHERE data @> '{"status":"active"}'` can use an index at scale. **Rule:** **almost always JSONB.** Use JSON only when you need to preserve the original text exactly (rare — auditing, signature verification). This is one of Postgres's killer features vs MySQL — schemaless data with real query performance.

**Q4: When would you use different index types?**

**A:** Postgres offers several: **B-tree** (default) — equality, range, ORDER BY. 90% of indexes. **Hash** — equality only; rarely worth it (B-tree usually as good and more flexible). **GIN (Generalized Inverted Index)** — great for arrays, JSONB, full-text search. Slow to update, fast to search. **GiST (Generalized Search Tree)** — for range types, geospatial (PostGIS), full-text with proximity. **SP-GiST** — for non-balanced data like phone directories. **BRIN (Block Range Index)** — tiny index for very large tables where rows correlate with insertion order (log data, time series). Only stores min/max per block range. **Bloom** — for equality lookups on multiple columns; probabilistic. **Rule:** default to B-tree. Reach for GIN when indexing JSONB / arrays / full-text. Everything else is niche.

**Q5: What is VACUUM and why does it matter?**

**A:** VACUUM reclaims space from dead row versions (MVCC leaves them behind). **Two flavors:** **VACUUM** — marks dead space as reusable (space stays allocated but future INSERTs use it). Cheap; runs online. **VACUUM FULL** — physically compacts the table. Locks the table for the entire operation — dangerous on production. **Autovacuum** — background process that runs VACUUM automatically based on table churn. Usually 'just works' but on write-heavy tables it can fall behind, causing bloat. **Tuning:** per-table `autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor` (default 0.2 = 20% of table churn triggers) — reduce to 0.05 for hot tables. Monitor with `pg_stat_user_tables`. **Emergency compaction:** `pg_repack` extension — online rebuild without long locks. **The one Postgres ops quirk everyone must understand.**

**Q6: How do you handle very large tables in Postgres?**

**A:** **Techniques in order:** **(1) Proper indexes** — verify with EXPLAIN; add composite/covering/partial as needed. **(2) `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY`** — add indexes without locking the table. **(3) Partitioning** — split by date (monthly partitions of events), by tenant (shard-ish), by range. Native declarative partitioning since PG10. Queries route to the right partition automatically. **(4) Read replicas** — offload analytics + reporting to replicas. **(5) BRIN indexes** — tiny indexes for time-correlated data (only min/max per block range). **(6) Materialized views** — pre-computed aggregates refreshed on schedule. **(7) TimescaleDB extension** — automatic time-series partitioning + compression. **When Postgres isn't enough:** columnar warehouse (ClickHouse, BigQuery), search DB (Elasticsearch), CDC to a specialized system. But a well-tuned Postgres routinely handles tens of TB.

**Q7: What are Postgres extensions and which are essential?**

**A:** Extensions add functionality WITHOUT modifying core. Loaded per-database with `CREATE EXTENSION`. **Essential extensions to know:** **`postgis`** — geospatial; one of the world's best GIS DBs. **`pg_trgm`** — trigram-based fuzzy text search (great for typo-tolerant lookups + LIKE '%foo%' with an index). **`uuid-ossp`** or built-in `gen_random_uuid()` — generate UUIDs. **`pgcrypto`** — hashing, encryption. **`hstore`** — key-value store type. **`pg_stat_statements`** — track slow queries automatically. **Ops-focused:** **`pg_partman`** (partitioning), **`pg_repack`** (online table compaction). **Analytics:** **TimescaleDB** (time-series), **`pgvector`** (embeddings for AI/RAG). **Rule:** know pg_stat_statements + pg_trgm + PostGIS exist. Reach for extensions instead of external systems whenever possible — one less operational burden.

## Real-world usage

- **Instagram, Reddit, Discord, Apple, Netflix, Robinhood, Notion** — Postgres at massive scale.
- **Modern startups + greenfield projects** in the 2020s default to Postgres.
- **Supabase, Neon, Crunchy Data, Aiven** — serverless / managed Postgres platforms.
- **AWS Aurora PostgreSQL** — Amazon's re-architected variant with separated storage.
- **PostGIS** — used by Uber, Airbnb, cartography apps worldwide.
- **RAG / AI apps** — pgvector is booming.
- **Timescale** — the go-to time-series option built on Postgres.
- **Not for:** heavy multi-terabyte analytics (BigQuery, ClickHouse), key-value at 1M+ QPS (Redis).

## Advanced notes

- **`pg_stat_statements`** — every DBA's first extension; find slow queries automatically.
- **`EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)`** — real execution timings + buffer hits.
- **Streaming replication** — WAL shipped to replicas continuously.
- **Logical replication** — replicate specific tables; enables blue/green migrations.
- **Point-in-time recovery** — WAL archiving lets you restore to any second.
- **`pg_basebackup`** — physical backup while running.
- **Foreign Data Wrappers (FDW)** — query external systems as tables (Postgres → MySQL, MongoDB, CSV, S3).
- **Row-level security** — declarative multi-tenancy at the DB layer.
- **Trigger functions** — audit tables, cascaded updates, custom constraints.
- **PL/pgSQL, PL/Python, PL/Rust, PL/JavaScript** — write stored procedures in your favorite language.
- **`pg_dump` + `pg_restore`** — logical backup + restore.
- **`pgbench`** — built-in load testing tool.
- **Zero-downtime migrations** — combine `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN` (instant since PG11) + `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` + gh-ost-style patterns.
- **`pg_hba.conf`** — client authentication config (a common source of 'why can't I connect?' problems).
- **Postgres versions** — active development; upgrade regularly. Major releases every year.

flashcards.back: JSONB = binary + indexable + rich operators (@>, ?, ->, ->>). Almost always use JSONB.
TIMESTAMPTZ stores UTC + converts on read (correct). TIMESTAMP has no timezone (ambiguous). Always TIMESTAMPTZ.
Every UPDATE keeps old version. Dead rows accumulate. VACUUM reclaims. Autovacuum runs automatically; tune per-table for high-write.
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY — no table lock during index creation. Essential for zero-downtime.
pg_stat_statements, pg_trgm, PostGIS, uuid-ossp (or gen_random_uuid()), pgvector.
One process per connection (~10MB). Use PgBouncer to fan-in from 10k app connections → 100 backend.

options: Dead row versions accumulate → VACUUM reclaims

challenges.text: Modern schema: users with JSONB metadata + array tags + TIMESTAMPTZ + BIGSERIAL.
GIN index on JSONB + measure @> query performance.
Full-text search with tsvector + GIN + plainto_tsquery.
Bloat investigation: load 1M rows, update 100k, tune autovacuum, verify.
Zero-downtime ADD COLUMN with default value + CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY.
Streaming replication: primary + replica in docker-compose; measure lag.
PostGIS: find all users within 5km of a lat/lng; index with GiST.
